content.problemStatement:
For a permutation ( P = (p_1, p_2, \dots, p_N) ) of numbers ([1, 2, \dots, N]), we define the function ( f(P) = \max(p_1, p_2) + \max(p_2, p_3) + \dots + \max(p_{N-1}, p_N) ).

You are given ( N ) and an integer ( K ). Find and report a permutation ( P ) of ([1, 2, \dots, N]) such that ( f(P) = K ), if such a permutation exists.

Note ( f([1]) = 0 ).


Input Format

The first line of the input contains an integer ( T ) denoting the number of test cases. The description of ( T ) test cases follows.
The only line of each test case consists of two space-separated integers ( N, K ) respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, if a permutation satisfying the condition exists, output a single line containing ( N ) space-separated integers which denotes any such permutation. If no such permutation exists, output a single integer -1 instead.


Use fast I/O methods since the size of the output is large.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases in each file <= 10^6
content.inputFormat: The first line of the input contains an integer T denoting the number of test cases. The description of T test cases follows. The only line of each test case consists of two space-separated integers N and K respectively.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, if a permutation satisfying the condition exists, output a single line containing N space-separated integers which denotes any such permutation. If no such permutation exists, output a single integer -1 instead.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Explanation: Example 1. There doesn't exist any permutation of numbers ([1, 2, 3, 4]) that can have its f value equal to 4. Hence answer is -1. Example 2. The permutations ([1, 2]) and ([2, 1]) both have their f values equal to 2. You can print any of these two permutations. Example 3. The permutation ([5, 4, 3, 2, 1]) has f value (= max(5, 4) + max(4, 3) + max(3, 2) + max(2, 1) = 5 + 4 + 3 + 2 = 14).

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we need to find a permutation of numbers from 1 to N such that the sum of the maximum of each pair of adjacent numbers equals K. We can start by checking if K is within the possible range for a given N. The minimum possible value of f(P) is 0 (when the permutation is in ascending order), and the maximum possible value is N*(N-1)/2 + (N-1) (when the permutation is in descending order). If K is within this range, we can try to construct a permutation that satisfies the condition.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T*N) — because for each test case, we are potentially generating a permutation of length N
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — because we need to store the permutation of length N
